content.text: Before defining a communications cadence, you must map stakeholders on two axes:
(their ability to shape programme decisions or cancel funding) and
(their desire for detailed programme information). The quadrant position determines the communication frequency, channel, and depth.
Over-communicating to low-interest stakeholders wastes their time and yours. Under-communicating to high-influence stakeholders creates surprise — and surprises at programme level always go badly.

attrs.alt: Stakeholder influence-interest quadrant map showing CTO/Sponsor and Delivery Director in manage-closely quadrant, Engineering Leads and Product in keep-informed quadrant
attrs.title: Stakeholder Communications Cadence Map

content.text: The map shows typical stakeholder positions for a GenAI programme. The
quadrant (high influence, high interest) requires the most intensive cadence: weekly briefings, same-day escalation, and proactive decision requests. The
quadrant (high influence, low interest) requires infrequent but high-quality communications at key events. The
quadrant (low influence, high interest) gets regular operational updates through lightweight channels like Slack and sprint reviews. The

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class InfluenceLevel(Enum):
    HIGH = "high"
    LOW  = "low"

class InterestLevel(Enum):
    HIGH = "high"
    LOW  = "low"

class CommsStrategy(Enum):
    MANAGE_CLOSELY  = "manage_closely"   # high influence, high interest
    KEEP_SATISFIED  = "keep_satisfied"   # high influence, low interest
    KEEP_INFORMED   = "keep_informed"    # low influence, high interest
    MONITOR         = "monitor"          # low influence, low interest

@dataclass
class Stakeholder:
    name: str
    role: str
    influence: InfluenceLevel
    interest: InterestLevel
    primary_concern: str         # what they care most about
    preferred_channel: str       # email, slack, meeting, report
    decision_authority: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # what they can approve

    @property
    def strategy(self) -> CommsStrategy:
        if self.influence == InfluenceLevel.HIGH and self.interest == InterestLevel.HIGH:
            return CommsStrategy.MANAGE_CLOSELY
        if self.influence == InfluenceLevel.HIGH and self.interest == InterestLevel.LOW:
            return CommsStrategy.KEEP_SATISFIED
        if self.influence == InfluenceLevel.LOW and self.interest == InterestLevel.HIGH:
            return CommsStrategy.KEEP_INFORMED
        return CommsStrategy.MONITOR


# Example stakeholder registry
STAKEHOLDERS = [
    Stakeholder(
        name="CTO", role="Programme Sponsor",
        influence=InfluenceLevel.HIGH, interest=InterestLevel.HIGH,
        primary_concern="Strategic value and risk",
        preferred_channel="1-page exec summary + steering meeting",
        decision_authority=["budget increases > £100k", "scope changes", "programme stop/go"],
    ),
    Stakeholder(
        name="Head of ML Engineering", role="Engineering Lead",
        influence=InfluenceLevel.LOW, interest=InterestLevel.HIGH,
        primary_concern="Technical feasibility and team capacity",
        preferred_channel="weekly standup + Slack",
        decision_authority=["technical architecture choices", "team allocation within squad"],
    ),
    Stakeholder(
        name="Chief Information Security Officer", role="InfoSec",
        influence=InfluenceLevel.HIGH, interest=InterestLevel.LOW,
        primary_concern="Security and compliance sign-off",
        preferred_channel="milestone-driven email with security summary",
        decision_authority=["security clearance for production deployment"],
    ),
]
Each comms strategy maps to a defined meeting type, frequency, channel, and content depth. Document these in a
at programme kick-off and share it with all stakeholders so they know what to expect and when.
1-page exec view: RAG, next milestone, ask

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date

class CommsFrequency(Enum):
    DAILY        = "daily"
    WEEKLY       = "weekly"
    BIWEEKLY     = "biweekly"
    MONTHLY      = "monthly"
    MILESTONE    = "milestone_only"
    AD_HOC       = "ad_hoc"

@dataclass
class CommunicationEvent:
    stakeholder: Stakeholder
    frequency: CommsFrequency
    channel: str
    content_template: str      # what sections to include
    owner: str                 # who prepares it
    next_due: date

@dataclass
class CommunicationsPlan:
    programme: str
    start_date: date
    events: list[CommunicationEvent] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add_event(self, event: CommunicationEvent) -> None:
        self.events.append(event)

    def events_due_this_week(self, week_start: date) -> list[CommunicationEvent]:
        from datetime import timedelta
        week_end = week_start + timedelta(days=6)
        return [e for e in self.events if week_start <= e.next_due <= week_end]

    def overdue_events(self, as_of: date) -> list[CommunicationEvent]:
        return [e for e in self.events if e.next_due < as_of]


def build_default_comms_plan(
    programme: str,
    stakeholders: list[Stakeholder],
    start_date: date,
) -> CommunicationsPlan:
    from datetime import timedelta
    plan = CommunicationsPlan(programme=programme, start_date=start_date)

    strategy_defaults = {
        CommsStrategy.MANAGE_CLOSELY: (CommsFrequency.WEEKLY,  "weekly status report",    "PM",            7),
        CommsStrategy.KEEP_SATISFIED: (CommsFrequency.MONTHLY, "1-page exec summary",     "PM + Dir",     30),
        CommsStrategy.KEEP_INFORMED:  (CommsFrequency.WEEKLY,  "highlights + blockers",   "Tech Lead",     7),
        CommsStrategy.MONITOR:        (CommsFrequency.BIWEEKLY,"single-para status",      "PM",           14),
    }
    for s in stakeholders:
        freq, template, owner, days = strategy_defaults[s.strategy]
        plan.add_event(CommunicationEvent(
            stakeholder=s,
            frequency=freq,
            channel=s.preferred_channel,
            content_template=template,
            owner=owner,
            next_due=start_date + timedelta(days=days),
        ))
    return plan

Visualise the communications cadence as a weekly calendar so the PM can spot overloaded weeks and plan prep time. A typical GenAI programme generates 4–6 communication touchpoints per week across all stakeholder groups.
The cadence above handles routine reporting. Exception communications — triggered by RAG changes, missed milestones, or budget alerts — are separate and must happen
the regular cadence, not deferred to the next scheduled meeting.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Callable

class ExceptionTrigger(Enum):
    RAG_CHANGE      = "rag_change"
    MILESTONE_MISS  = "milestone_miss"
    BUDGET_ALERT    = "budget_alert"
    BLOCKED         = "blocked"
    SCOPE_CHANGE    = "scope_change"

@dataclass
class ExceptionCommsRule:
    trigger: ExceptionTrigger
    threshold: str              # condition that fires the rule
    target_audience: list[str]
    channel: str
    max_response_hours: float   # how quickly the PM must communicate

EXCEPTION_RULES: list[ExceptionCommsRule] = [
    ExceptionCommsRule(
        trigger=ExceptionTrigger.RAG_CHANGE,
        threshold="Any status change to AMBER, RED, or BLOCKED",
        target_audience=["Delivery Director"],
        channel="Slack + email",
        max_response_hours=4,
    ),
    ExceptionCommsRule(
        trigger=ExceptionTrigger.RAG_CHANGE,
        threshold="Status change to RED or BLOCKED",
        target_audience=["CTO", "Delivery Director"],
        channel="Phone + email",
        max_response_hours=1,
    ),
    ExceptionCommsRule(
        trigger=ExceptionTrigger.MILESTONE_MISS,
        threshold="Any integration milestone slips by > 3 days",
        target_audience=["Delivery Director", "Programme Sponsor"],
        channel="Email + calendar invite for emergency meeting",
        max_response_hours=8,
    ),
    ExceptionCommsRule(
        trigger=ExceptionTrigger.BUDGET_ALERT,
        threshold="Budget variance exceeds 10% of quarter forecast",
        target_audience=["CFO", "Delivery Director"],
        channel="Email with FinOps dashboard link",
        max_response_hours=24,
    ),
    ExceptionCommsRule(
        trigger=ExceptionTrigger.SCOPE_CHANGE,
        threshold="Any change to committed scope or delivery date",
        target_audience=["CTO", "Programme Sponsor", "CFO"],
        channel="Steering meeting (ad-hoc) + written change request",
        max_response_hours=48,
    ),
]

def get_rules_for_trigger(trigger: ExceptionTrigger) -> list[ExceptionCommsRule]:
    return [r for r in EXCEPTION_RULES if r.trigger == trigger]
Adjusting Cadence Through the Programme Lifecycle

A fixed cadence for the full programme duration is inefficient. Early phases (discovery, design) require less frequent sponsor engagement; late phases (integration testing, go-live) require more. Define cadence breakpoints at the start of the programme and communicate them in the Communications Plan.
: bi-weekly steering; weekly engineering standup. Sponsor engagement is light because decisions are not yet needed at that level.
: weekly steering briefing; daily engineering standup. RAG reporting begins. Integration milestone tracking starts.
: twice-weekly delivery director briefing; daily engineering standup; weekly executive summary. Highest risk period.
: daily CTO briefing; twice-weekly all-stakeholder update until post-launch steady-state is confirmed.
: sending the full 5-section weekly report to the CTO burns their time and trains them to stop reading it. Tailor depth to audience.
Cadence without a Communications Plan document
: stakeholders don't know when to expect updates and chase the PM ad-hoc, fragmenting attention. Publish the cadence calendar at kick-off.
Exception comms delayed to the next scheduled meeting
: a RED status found on Thursday should not wait until Monday's standup. Exception triggers have their own response windows.
: intensive go-live communication cadences running for the full programme exhaust stakeholders and cause them to disengage precisely when it matters.

A cadence that nobody reads or acts on is overhead, not communication. Measure effectiveness quarterly using three proxies: decision response time, stakeholder attendance rate at scheduled meetings, and the ratio of proactive updates to reactive queries.
Days from decision request to approval
Escalate to next level; simplify the ask
% of required attendees who join scheduled meetings
Reschedule or convert to async format

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta

@dataclass
class CommsEffectivenessMetrics:
    programme: str
    quarter: str
    total_decisions_requested: int
    decisions_responded_within_3d: int
    meetings_scheduled: int
    meetings_with_full_attendance: int
    proactive_updates_sent: int
    reactive_queries_received: int
    reports_sent: int
    reports_acknowledged: int

    @property
    def decision_response_rate(self) -> float:
        if not self.total_decisions_requested:
            return 1.0
        return self.decisions_responded_within_3d / self.total_decisions_requested

    @property
    def attendance_rate(self) -> float:
        if not self.meetings_scheduled:
            return 1.0
        return self.meetings_with_full_attendance / self.meetings_scheduled

    @property
    def proactive_ratio(self) -> float:
        total = self.proactive_updates_sent + self.reactive_queries_received
        if not total:
            return 1.0
        return self.proactive_updates_sent / total

    @property
    def report_read_rate(self) -> float:
        if not self.reports_sent:
            return 1.0
        return self.reports_acknowledged / self.reports_sent

    def health_summary(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "programme": self.programme,
            "quarter": self.quarter,
            "decision_response_rate": f"{self.decision_response_rate:.0%}",
            "attendance_rate": f"{self.attendance_rate:.0%}",
            "proactive_ratio": f"{self.proactive_ratio:.0%}",
            "report_read_rate": f"{self.report_read_rate:.0%}",
            "needs_review": (
                self.decision_response_rate < 0.7 or
                self.attendance_rate < 0.8 or
                self.proactive_ratio < 0.7
            ),
        }


# Example for Q1 2025
metrics = CommsEffectivenessMetrics(
    programme="genai-customer-copilot",
    quarter="Q1-2025",
    total_decisions_requested=8,
    decisions_responded_within_3d=6,
    meetings_scheduled=12,
    meetings_with_full_attendance=10,
    proactive_updates_sent=18,
    reactive_queries_received=5,
    reports_sent=12,
    reports_acknowledged=9,
)
import pprint
pprint.pprint(metrics.health_summary())
# {'programme': 'genai-customer-copilot',
#  'quarter': 'Q1-2025',
#  'decision_response_rate': '75%',
#  'attendance_rate': '83%',
#  'proactive_ratio': '78%',
#  'report_read_rate': '75%',
#  'needs_review': False}

at the end of each quarter and review with the delivery director. Metrics below target trigger a cadence retrospective — adjust frequency, channel, or content depth based on stakeholder feedback rather than guessing.
A stakeholder communications cadence is a

about who receives what information, through which channel, and how often. Map stakeholders by influence and interest to assign one of four strategies (manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, monitor). Define exception rules that fire outside the regular cadence for RAG changes, missed milestones, and budget alerts. Adjust cadence intensity as the programme moves from discovery through go-live — matching communication overhead to actual decision-making needs.
